title: Voltammetry Without Adding Supporting Electrolyte Using a Solid Polymer Electrolyte Composite Microelectrode
abstract:
Voltammetry Without Adding Supporting Electrolyte Using a Solid
Polymer Electrolyte Composite Microelectrode
Jun-Hua Jiang,*+,++ Bing-Liang Wu,+ Chuan-Sin Cha,+ and Run-Sheng Zhai++
+Department of Chemistry, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, P. R. China
++ State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Dalian 116023, P. R. China
Received: October 17, 1997
Final version: January 26, 1998
Abstract
Well-developed voltammetry with good reproducibility can be easily obtained without adding supporting electrolyte by use of a solid polymer
electrolyte (SPE) composite microdisk electrode, which is made of a microdisk electrode and a SPE membrane. The measured current is dependent
on the dissolution and transport rate of electroactive species in the SPE membrane. If the dissolution of the species in the membrane can be
expressed by Henry’s law, the current is proportional to the concentration of the species in organic solution. The linear relationship shows the
potential applications of SPE composite microelectrodes in the analysis of the electroactive species in organic media.
Keywords: Solid polymer electrolyte, Voltammetry, Composite microelectrode, Electroactive species, Supporting electrolyte
1. Introduction
The solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) has been the subject
of
interest
in
a
variety
of
electrochemical
applications,
including electrolysis [1], fuel cells [2], surface modiﬁcation
[3, 4], electrochemical synthesis [5, 6], electrocatalysis [7–9] and
electrochemical sensors [10, 11]. Naﬁon(Du Pont), a perﬂuorated
cation-exchange membrane, is one of the best known SPE
materials and has been extensively studied [12, 13]. Takenaka
and Torikai reported that a metal-SPE electrode could be
conveniently fabricated by chemical reduction (or electroless
deposition) method [14]. Alternatively, a metal mesh electrode is
attached to one surface of an SPE membrane. The resulting
electrode–membrane assembly separates the analyte solution (in
contact with the metal electrode) and an electrolyte solution in
which the counter and reference electrodes are placed. No
supporting electrolyte is required in the analyte because ions
consumed or produced at the electrode can be transported through
the SPE membrane rather than the analyte solution itself. Two
types of electrode–membrane assemblies, platinum mesh partially
embedded in a membrane of Naﬁon 117 and a chemically deposited
porous platinum layer on the membrane, were used for cyclic
voltammetry of species dissolved in water, n-hexane and aceto-
nitrile with no supporting electrolyte [15].
However, the mesh–Naﬁon assemblies are unstable with respect
to separation of the mesh from the membrane. The great apparent
surface area for a chemically deposited M-Naﬁon assembly
usually leads to the deviation of electrochemical information
from cyclic voltammograms. The use of microelectrodes is
especially promising. Different SPE composite electrodes, includ-
ing the SPE composite microdisk electrode, the SPE composite
powder microelectrode and the open powder layer–SPE compo-
site microelectrode, were developed in our laboratory in recent
years [16–18]. Because of the thickness of about 0.18 mm for a
commercial Naﬁon 117 membrane, if the diffusion of the species in
the membrane is not too slow or not fast enough, the semi-inﬁnite
diffusion condition still holds. The voltammogram characters at
the composite microelectrodes are similar to those at usual
microelectrodes in a conventional cell. The pathway of mass
transport of electroactive compounds in the vincinity of the
interface wass investigated by use of these microelectrode
technique [19]. It was found that electroactive compounds in the
bulk solution were transported through the porous electrocatalyst
layer followed by their dissolution in the SPE membrane, then
diffusing in the membrane to the electrode surface at the steady-
state condition. This result provides theoretical basis for applying
SPE composite microelectrode to the analysis of the electroactive
species in the organic solution without adding supporting electro-
lyte. Such possibility is illustrated below for the analysis of
nitrobenzene-methanol solution in the absence of supporting
electrolyte.
2. Experimental
2.1. Chemical Reagents and Instruments
Nitrobenzene and methanol were of analytical grade and used
without further puriﬁcation. Aqueous solution was prepared from
double distilled water.
Cyclic voltammograms were performed with an AFRDE5
potentiostat (Pine Company) and a 3086 X–Y recorder (Sichuan
Electric Instrumental Corp., China).
2.2. Cu-Naﬁon Assembly
As received Nafon 117 membrane (Du Pont) was pretreated
in 3 % peroxide hydrogen and 1 mol L–1 sulfuric acid to remove
impurities and then sonicated in double distilled water. The Cu
microdisk electrode (diameter of 116 mm) was prepared according
to usual thermal-sealed procedure. This microelectrode was
polished and sonicated in acetone and double distilled water
before every measurement. The water adherent to the surface of
the membrane was removed before it was put into the electrolysis
cell. The microelectrode was pressed against the membrane by a
device to form the Cu-Naﬁon composite microelectrode.
2.3. Cell for Voltammetry
The cell for voltammetry was described in detail in our previous
work [16]. The cell was composed of two compartments which
were separated by the Naﬁon membrane. The upper working
electrode compartment was ﬁlled with nitrobenzene-methanol
solution, while the lower counter electrode compartment was
ﬁlled with 0.1 mol L–1 hydrochloric acid, in which a Ag/AgCl
electrode served as reference electrode.
343
Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
q WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1998
1040-0397/98/0504-0343 $ 17.50þ.50/0
 2.4. Voltammetry Measurements
The ﬁrst procedure was to add directly nitrobenzene-methanol
solution into the working electrode compartment after the mem-
brane was placed in the electrolysis cell. After 5 min, the electrode
was contacted with the membrane. The electrode was ﬁrst polarized
for 3 min at –0.30 V (vs. Ag/AgCl, the same below) before each
run, then the potential was scanned to the upper limit and reversed.
Cyclic voltammograms for the ﬁrst scan were recorded during
each run.
The second procedure was to put the membrane into the
nitrobenzene-methanol solution for 4 h, which was pretreated at
80 8C for 20 min to remove some water. Some amount of
nitrobenzene dissolved in the membrane. The membrane was
taken out and was rinsed immediately in pure methanol solvent
to remove the nitrobenzene adherent to the surface of the
membrane. After the methanol adherent to the surface was
removed, the membrane was placed immediately into the electro-
lysis cell. The working compartment was ﬁlled with nothing but
air. The Cu microelectrode was contacted rapidly with the
membrane. The electrode was also ﬁrst polarized at –0.3 V for
3 min before each run.
3. Results and Discussion
Figure 1 shows the cyclic voltammograms for 3 % (J, the same
below) nitrobenzene-methanol solution at the Cu microdisk
electrode–Naﬁon membrane interface (Fig. 1a) and the relation
between Ip and the scan rate (n) (Fig. 1b). During the potential
scan only one obvious reduction peak appears at about –0.68 V.
Ip is proportional to n1/2. The steady-state polarization curve for
3 % nitrobenzene-methanol solution at the interface is shown in
Figure 2. The peak current is dependent upon the concentration
and diffusion coefﬁcient of nitrobenzene in the Naﬁon membrane.
The linear relationship of Ip–n1/2 for an irreversible reaction at a
planar electrode can be described as [20]
Ip ¼ 2:99 × 105 nðanÞ1=2AD1=2
appn1=2c00
ð1Þ
where Dapp is the apparent diffusion coefﬁcient of the electroactive
species, A is the geometric surface area of the electrode and c00 is
the concentration of the species; other terms have their usual
meanings. The linear Ip–n1/2 plot produces a slope (k),
k ¼ 2:99 × 105nðanÞ1=2AD1=2
appc00
ð2Þ
The limiting steady-state current (Id), not depending on n, is given
by [21]
Id ¼ 4nFDappc00r
ð3Þ
where r is the radius of the microdisk.
In case that the electrons involved in the reaction corresponding
to the peak current are the same as those corresponding to the
limiting steady-state current, combining Equation 2 and Equation 3
results in
Dapp ¼ 5:92anr 2I 2
d=k 2
ð4Þ
where an can be determined according to the Tafel slope of the
steady-state polarization curve. Dapp can be obtained according
to Equation 4. Once Dapp is known, c00 can be calculated from
Equation 3.
Dapp and c00
are measured to be 7.3 × 10–7 cm2 s–1 and
0.061 mol L–1 for nitrobenzene in the Naﬁon membrane, respec-
tively. This concentration value is far less than that in the bulk
solution (0.291 mol L–1). Dapp is larger than some reported
values of some species in the recast Naﬁon membrane (10–8–
10–12 cm2 s–1) which depends on many factors [22, 23]. The
enhanced Dapp implies that nitrobenzene can diffuse faster in the
commercial Naﬁon membrane containing the methanol solvent.
Nitrobenzene can combine methanol molecules to form a layer
of solvent-shell. The solvent-shell may decrease the interaction
between nitrobenzene and Naﬁon. Methanol itself can move
relatively fast in the membrane, the diffusion coefﬁcient reaches
1.15 × 10–5 cm2 s–1 [24]. In addition, the membrane swells greatly
in methanol. The swollen membrane is favorable to the diffusion
of nitrobenzene in the membrane. The enhanced Dapp is necessary
to improve the ratio of the faradaic to background current.
The dissolution of liquid compounds in the polymer is similar to
that of gases in the polymer [25]. If no interaction occurs between
the polymer and the solute, the dissolution can be exempliﬁed by
the Henry’s law,
c 00 ¼ Hc 0
ð5Þ
where c00 and c0 are the concentration of the solute in the polymer
and that in the bulk solution, respectively; H is a constant not
depending on the concentration. H can be determined by the
measured c00 and known c0 according to Equation 5. Reversibly,
the concentration of the solute in the bulk solution can be obtained
from H and the measured c00.
The dependence of the peak current (Ip) on the concentration
of nitrobenzene in the bulk solution was measured. Ip is approxi-
mately proportional to c0 at c0 < 10 %. When c0 exceeds 10 %,
Ip deviates below the linear relation. The linearity indicates that
the dissolution of nitrobenzene in the membrane can be expressed
by Henry’s law at the low concentration range. H is considered
to remain constant at the range of low concentration.
The second procedure is to immerse the Naﬁon membrane in the
nitrobenzene-methanol solution with different concentrations for

at the Cu–Naﬁon interface (n ¼ 100 mV s–1) (a) and the dependence of Ip on

Electroanalysis 1998, 10, No. 5
Fig. 1. Cyclic voltammograms for 3 % (J) nitrobenzene-methanol solution
at the Cu–Naﬁon interface (n ¼ 100 mV s–1) (a) and the dependence of Ip on
Fig. 2. Steady-state polarization curve for 3 % nitrobenzene-methanol at the
Cu–Naﬁon interface (n ¼ 1 mV s–1).
4 h. Nitrobenzene can dissolve in the micropores of the membrane.
The concentration of nitrobenzene dissolved in the membrane
depends on that in the immersing solution. Once the dissolution
equilibrium is established, the concentration of nitrobenzene in the
membrane remains constant. The cyclic voltammograms for
nitrobenzene loaded ﬁrst in the membrane are shown in Figure 3.
No obvious reduction peak appears in the base CV. When the
membrane loads some nitrobenzene, there was a reduction peak
at about –0.68 V. The curve is similar in shape to that shown in
Figure 1. At this condition, a current–time transient associated
with the nitrobenzene reduction at the Cu–Naﬁon interface is
shown in Figure 4a. The current does not decay to zero at the
increasingly polarized time and ﬁnally remains almost constant. A
typical I–t –1/2 plot for such data is shown in Figure 4b. The ﬁnal
steady-state current wave and linear I–t –1/2 relation indicate that
the electrode reaction is diffusion-controlled. The character was
different from that of a Naﬁon-coated microelectrode. The latter
usually produced voltammograms typical for a surface adsorbed
The peak current depends on the concentration of nitrobenzene
dissolved ﬁrst in the membrane. The latter is determined by the
concentration of nitrobenzene in the immersing solution (c0). The
peak current was almost proportional to c0 at c0 < 20 %. This linear
relation indicates that the concentration of nitrobenzene dissolved
in the membrane is proportional to that in the immersing solution
and the dissolution of nitrobenzene can be exempliﬁed by Henry’s
law. The linear scope was enhanced.
The main advantage of SPE composite electrodes is that no
supporting electrolyte is required. The concentration of the species
measured with SPE composite electrodes is that dissolved in the
membrane. If the dissolution of some species in the membrane
can be described by Henry’s law, the concentration of the species
in the membrane is proportional to that in the analyte solution. The
linearity enables the analyte to be measured using SPE composite
electrodes. The upper detection limit is dependent upon the
dissolution of the analyte in the membrane.
The SPE composite microelectrode made of a microelectrode and
a commercial Naﬁon membrane has higher stability and better
reproducibility than a usual Naﬁon-coated microelectrode. In
addition, voltammetric measurements can be carried out without
adding a supporting electrolyte. Although voltammetric measure-
ments can also be carried out with microelectrodes in many organic
solvents in the absence of an intentionally added supporting
electrolyte, a ultramicroelectrode (<1 mm) is required to provide
undistorted voltammograms [27]. The preparation of the ultra-
microelectrode is more difﬁcult. The shape of the voltammograms
is distorted greatly by high resistance without adding supporting
electrolyte at a usual microelectrode (>10 mm) [28]. The SPE
composite microelectrodes have none of these limits. Moreover,
some sample solutions with high concentration can be measured
directly by use of the composite microelectrode.
The SPE composite microelectrode thus represents an analyti-
cally useful device. However, it should be pointed out that SPE
composite electrodes are invalid for the analysis of some species
whose dissolution in the membrane can not be exempliﬁed by
